edges.description: Portal microbial products stimulate hepatic innate immune cells.
edges.source: Gut-Liver Axis Barrier Disruption and Dysbiosis

edges.description: Innate inflammatory activation injures the biliary epithelium.
Aberrant immune trafficking sustains bile-duct injury.
Dysregulated bile-acid signaling contributes to cholestasis.
Bile-acid dysregulation can promote inflammation and fibrosis.
Senescent cholangiocyte signaling promotes periductal fibrosis.

edges.target: Gut-Liver Axis Barrier Disruption and Dysbiosis

nodes.description: Gut-primed lymphocytes aberrantly home to the liver and biliary tree, sustaining antigen-driven cholangiocyte injury in PSC-associated gut-liver-axis disease.

evidence.reference_title: Gut–Liver Axis, Microbiota, Bile Acids, and Immune Response in Pathogenesis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis: An Overview
evidence.snippet: Concurrently, gut-primed lymphocytes expressing mucosal homing receptors migrate aberrantly to the liver, where they may contribute to biliary epithelial cell injury.

nodes.description: Dysregulated bile-acid receptor signaling in cholangiocytes and the gut-liver axis promotes cholestasis, inflammation, and fibrotic remodeling.

evidence.reference_title: Gut–Liver Axis, Microbiota, Bile Acids, and Immune Response in Pathogenesis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis: An Overview
evidence.snippet: Moreover, bile acids act as signaling molecules, regulating metabolism and immune responses through receptors such as FXR and TGR5. Dysregulation of these pathways may promote cholestasis, inflammation, and fibrosis.

evidence.reference_title: Bile Acids-Based Therapies for Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis: Current Landscape and Future Developments
evidence.snippet: Recent studies have revealed that these cells undergo a downregulation of GPBAR1 (TGR5), a bile acid receptor involved in bicarbonate secretion and immune regulation.

nodes.description: Fibrotic remodeling of affected bile ducts narrows the duct lumen, producing strictures and downstream cholestasis.

evidence.reference_title: IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis and Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis.
evidence.snippet: Sclerosing cholangitis (SC) is defined as a condition with progressive stenosis and destruction of the bile ducts due to diffuse inflammation and fibrosis and currently includes three categories: primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), secondary cholangitis, and IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis (IgG4-SC).

nodes.description: Inflammatory injury of the bile-duct epithelium and duct wall is a shared upstream feature across sclerosing cholangitis categories.

evidence.reference_title: IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis and Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis.
evidence.snippet: Sclerosing cholangitis (SC) is defined as a condition with progressive stenosis and destruction of the bile ducts due to diffuse inflammation and fibrosis and currently includes three categories: primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), secondary cholangitis, and IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis (IgG4-SC).

nodes.description: Stressed cholangiocytes can acquire a senescence-associated secretory phenotype that is proinflammatory and profibrogenic, driving crosstalk with portal fibroblasts and hepatic stellate cells.

evidence.reference_title: Central role for cholangiocyte pathobiology in cholestatic liver diseases.
evidence.snippet: The proclivity of these cells to undergo a senescence-associated secretory phenotype, which is proinflammatory and profibrogenic, and the intrinsic intracellular activation pathways resulting in the secretion of cytokines and chemokines are reviewed.

nodes.description: Persistent obstruction and cholestasis from bile-duct strictures drive cholestatic liver injury and can progress to biliary cirrhosis.

evidence.reference_title: Central role for cholangiocyte pathobiology in cholestatic liver diseases.
evidence.snippet: Cholangiopathies comprise a spectrum of chronic intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary tract disorders culminating in progressive cholestatic liver injury, fibrosis, and often cirrhosis and its sequela.

evidence.reference_title: Central role for cholangiocyte pathobiology in cholestatic liver diseases.
evidence.snippet: Cholangiopathies comprise a spectrum of chronic intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary tract disorders culminating in progressive cholestatic liver injury, fibrosis, and often cirrhosis and its sequela.

nodes.description: ERCP can be used diagnostically and therapeutically in dominant or clinically relevant strictures, including dilation or stenting when appropriate.

evidence.reference_title: The 2024 diagnostic criteria for primary sclerosing cholangitis
evidence.snippet: The 2024 diagnostic criteria facilitate the use of magnetic resonance cholangiography in addition to endoscopic retrograde cholangiography in imaging, and incorporate gamma-glutamyl transferase for evaluating cholestasis to diagnose pediatric patients.

nodes.description: FMT is an investigational gut-microbiome-directed intervention for PSC, particularly in PSC with concomitant IBD.

evidence.reference_title: Fecal Microbiota Transplantation for the Treatment of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis.
evidence.snippet: This is an open-label single-arm pilot study to measure the safety, microbiological and clinical impacts of Fecal Microbiota Transplantation (FMT) in patients with Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC).

nodes.description: In primary sclerosing cholangitis, intestinal barrier disruption and dysbiosis permit microbial products to reach the portal circulation and amplify hepatobiliary immune activation.
nodes.id: Gut-Liver Axis Barrier Disruption and Dysbiosis

evidence.reference_title: Gut–Liver Axis, Microbiota, Bile Acids, and Immune Response in Pathogenesis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis: An Overview
evidence.snippet: In particular, disruption of the intestinal barrier allows microbial products to enter the portal circulation, stimulating hepatic immune cells and triggering biliary inflammation.

evidence.reference_title: Gut–Liver Axis, Microbiota, Bile Acids, and Immune Response in Pathogenesis of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis: An Overview
evidence.snippet: Dysbiosis, characterized by reduced microbial diversity and the expansion of bile-tolerant and pro-inflammatory taxa, amplifies this immune activation and disturbs gut–liver homeostasis.

nodes.description: Microbial and cholestatic signals activate cholangiocytes and liver macrophages, producing inflammatory cytokine and chemokine signaling in the biliary tree.

evidence.reference_title: Central role for cholangiocyte pathobiology in cholestatic liver diseases.
evidence.snippet: The crosstalk between cholangiocytes and cells of the innate (neutrophils and macrophages) and adaptive (T cells and B cells) immune systems is also examined in detail.

evidence.reference_title: Central role for cholangiocyte pathobiology in cholestatic liver diseases.
evidence.snippet: Cholangiopathies comprise a spectrum of chronic intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary tract disorders culminating in progressive cholestatic liver injury, fibrosis, and often cirrhosis and its sequela.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is used for end-stage or otherwise advanced sclerosing cholangitis when progressive liver failure or complications cannot be controlled.

evidence.snippet: Most patients eventually require liver transplantation or develop serious complications, the most severe being end-stage liver disease and cholangiocarcinoma.

nodes.description: norUDCA is an investigational bile-acid therapy in phase 3 testing for PSC.

evidence.reference_title: Double-blind, Randomized, Placebo-controlled, Phase III Study Comparing norUrsodeoxycholic Acid Capsules With Placebo in the Treatment of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis
evidence.snippet: Double-blind, randomized, multi-center, placebo-controlled, comparative, phase III trial. The study will be conducted with two treatment groups in the form of a parallel group comparison and will serve to compare oral treatment with either 1500 mg/d norursodeoxycholic acid capsules or placebo capsules for the treatment of Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis.
